findings.description: Table text formatting and hierarchy are weakened in the HTML conversion.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, the title is centered and visually distinct, and column headers are compactly grouped with clearer emphasis. In the HTML, the title/header area appears smaller and less structured, and table headers/body text have flatter styling, reducing visual distinction though content remains present.

findings.description: The HTML includes fabricated webpage boilerplate and styling content not present in the source PDF.
findings.evidence: HTML contains added content such as "Converted Document", CSS comments "Digital Commons NC — Standalone Theme CSS", and imported font/style definitions. The PDF is a single-page table image with no title tag, no CSS, and no web-theme text.

findings.description: The table header structure is not faithfully preserved; the PDF has multi-row grouped headers, while the HTML flattens and merges them into a single confusing row, losing exact structure.
findings.evidence: PDF header shows separate grouped rows: "Sampling Dates and Sample ID", date columns "2/14/2014", "2/17/2014", "2/26/2014", "2/27/2014", plus nested labels like "Report #", "Lab", and "Dissolved" sample IDs. HTML collapses these into one row with cells like "2/14/2014 Report # AC04986 Lab AC04987 Dissolved AC04993 Value" and "Qual".

findings.description: At least one header cell is mistranscribed, altering column labeling.
findings.evidence: PDF column header reads "Units\Hg 1631 PQL Units" as separate stacked labels around the Lab PQL/Units area, while HTML renders a merged header cell as "Units\Hg 1631", which does not accurately reflect the original structure.

findings.description: Heading structure is not represented with semantic heading tags; the document title/subtitle are plain bold paragraphs instead of headings.
findings.evidence:
PDF prominently displays title lines "Data from Ash Release at Duke Energy Dan River Station", "Stormwater Discharge Upstream of Ash Pond - 009", and "Sampling Dates and Sample ID" as document headings. HTML places them inside a single

...

block and uses no h1-h6 headings in the document body.

findings.description: Several textual inaccuracies and OCR/transcription errors are present in the HTML footnotes and labels.
findings.evidence: HTML footnote says "annlyzed" instead of PDF "analyzed"; "Reccomended" instead of "Recommended"; row label "Stronium Dissolved" instead of PDF "Strontium Dissolved". These are visible textual errors not attributable to simple formatting.
